_meta.note: Quiz-to-read-a-book tone · curly apostrophes only · glossary locked

title_001: A Journey to Mujin — Dopamine Master Quiz · Kim Seung-ok
html_001: Kim Seung-ok · 1964 · Sasanggye · the peak of the short story, called a revolution of sensibility
html_002: A few days in a hometown of fog, and a shameful return
html_003: A Journey to Mujin Master Quiz
html_004: “Mujin isn’t without a specialty. That specialty is fog.”
html_005: — from the novel A Journey to Mujin
html_006: You’ll master the characters, symbols, and themes of this novel set in the foggy town of Mujin through
html_009: every question you miss comes back until you get it right.
html_026: That’s the end of the free preview
html_029: One who saw through the fog
html_039: A Journey to Mujin at a Glance
html_040: String the whole novel onto one journey —
html_042: — and you’ll never forget it. What wraps around everything is
html_046: 💼 Boredom in Seoul, on the verge of success
html_047: A brief escape before a promotion to director
html_049: 🌫️ Return home to Mujin, city of fog
html_050: A hometown he goes to at his wife’s urging
html_052: 🎶 People met at night · Ha In-suk
html_053: Cho · Mr. Park · Tears of Mokpo
html_055: 🌊 A corpse on the seawall · one night · a promise to go to Seoul
html_056: A hazy escape and a fleeting sincerity
html_058: 📮 The wife’s telegram · tearing the letter and heading to Seoul
html_061: Table of Contents at a Glance
html_065: Focused Review of What You Missed
html_067: lets you explore the era behind A Journey to Mujin and Kim Seung-ok’s literary world in more depth.
html_073: A repeat master quiz that drills a book’s core with dopamine
html_074: This app is secondary learning content that reworks the book’s key ideas for study. For the full reading experience, seek out the original work.
html_075: A Journey to Mujin · Book info

js_004: Who wrote A Journey to Mujin?
js_009: A Journey to Mujin is a short story Kim Seung-ok published in 1964. With his sensory style, he opened new ground for modern Korean fiction.
js_011: Which best describes this work’s genre?
js_015: A lyric poem written in meter
js_016: A Journey to Mujin is a short story. Published in the magazine Sasanggye in 1964, it’s a short but deeply resonant masterpiece by Kim Seung-ok.
js_018: In which magazine did the novel first appear in 1964?
js_023: A Journey to Mujin appeared in Sasanggye in October 1964. It stirred a strong response from the moment it was published.
js_025: What’s the name of the first-person protagonist who leads the novel?
js_030: The protagonist and narrator is Yun Hui-jung, a man around thirty who quietly recounts his few days in Mujin through his own eyes.
js_032: The hometown the protagonist visits, a town famous for its fog, is?
js_037: The protagonist’s hometown is Mujin, a city of fog. It’s a fictional place that doesn’t really exist, modeled on the author’s hometown of Suncheon.
js_039: What does the novel name as Mujin’s local specialty?
js_044: Mujin has no real specialty; if anything, it’s the fog. That thick fog is the core symbol wrapping the whole novel.
js_046: Which is the right reason the protagonist went down to Mujin?
js_047: To rest a while at his wife’s urging, before a promotion
js_048: To flee to his beloved hometown after his business collapsed
js_049: To be at his aging mother’s deathbed
js_050: To hold a wedding with an old lover
js_051: On the verge of a promotion to director, the protagonist goes down to Mujin for a short rest at his wife’s urging — a brief escape before climbing the ladder.
js_053: What kind of company does the protagonist work at in Seoul?
js_058: The protagonist works at a pharmaceutical company in Seoul — one owned by his wife’s family, where he’s about to become an executive.
js_060: What position is the protagonist set to rise to after his trip to Mujin?
js_065: The protagonist is set to be promoted to managing director of his in-laws’ company. That climb is what calls him back from Mujin to reality.
js_067: The name of the music teacher the protagonist meets at a drinking party in Mujin and is drawn to?
js_072: The woman the protagonist is drawn to is Ha In-suk, a music teacher at Mujin Middle School. She longs desperately to leave Mujin for Seoul.
js_079: Encyclopedia of Korean Culture “A Journey to Mujin”
js_080: Digital Suncheon Culture Encyclopedia “Kim Seung-ok’s Journey to Mujin”
js_081: Kim Seung-ok (1941– ) debuted in 1962 and, with his sensory style, portrayed the urbanization and human alienation of the 1960s. A Journey to Mujin and Seoul, Winter 1964 are his signature works.
js_082: A Journey to Mujin is a short piece, yet through a few days in a fog-bound city it renders in depth the inner life of a man wavering between the ideal and reality.
js_083: A Journey to Mujin appeared in the magazine Sasanggye in October 1964. Ever since, it has been counted among the peaks of the modern Korean short story.
js_084: The narrator, Yun Hui-jung, is a man around thirty. Through his first-person eyes, a few hazy, fog-like days in Mujin unfold quietly.
js_085: Mujin is a fictional city that doesn’t really exist. Modeled on the author’s hometown of Suncheon in South Jeolla, it was created as a fog-covered symbolic space.
js_086: Mujin has no notable specialty; if anything, it’s the fog. That thick fog wraps the whole work, creating an atmosphere of reverie and escape.
js_087: On the verge of a promotion to director, the protagonist goes down to Mujin for a short rest at his wife’s urging. That brief escape before success becomes the story’s starting point.
js_088: The protagonist works at a pharmaceutical company owned by his wife’s family. This position, gained through marriage, is the tie that binds him back to reality.
js_089: The protagonist is set to become managing director of his in-laws’ company. His escapade in Mujin ends, in the end, before this rise to success.
js_090: Ha In-suk is a music teacher at Mujin Middle School. Toward her, longing to get out to Seoul, the protagonist feels both pity and attraction.
js_104: Mujin’s only specialty. Enveloping the whole city and blurring reality, it’s the work’s core image, symbolizing reverie, loss of self, and escape.
js_106: A fictional city whose name means ‘fog ferry.’ Modeled on the author’s hometown of Suncheon, it’s a symbolic space where ideal, unconscious, and escape entangle.
js_107: The first-person narrator and protagonist. A man around thirty on the verge of success, he wavers between the ideal (Mujin) and reality (Seoul).
js_108: A music teacher who majored in vocal music at a Seoul conservatory. Longing to leave Mujin for Seoul, she sings the popular song Tears of Mokpo at a drinking party.
js_110: The protagonist’s middle-school classmate and Mujin’s tax-office chief. He’s drawn as the face of snobbish worldliness — the local boy who made good.
js_112: A guileless junior teacher of Korean at his old school. Secretly in love with Ha In-suk, he’s an untainted figure set against Cho.
js_114: The wife’s telegram calling him to Seoul is reality’s summons; the letter he writes to Ha In-suk and then tears up is a folded-away sincerity. Two slips of paper decide the ending.
js_116: The achievement of Kim Seung-ok’s writing, portraying 1960s urbanization and human alienation in a sensory style. It’s called the expression that changed the course of modern Korean fiction.
js_119: Leaving Seoul, the protagonist heads for fog-wrapped Mujin.
js_120: It’s a trip he takes at his wife’s urging, before a promotion to director.
js_121: The closer the bus gets to Mujin, the thicker the fog settles.
js_122: Boredom on the edge of success and a hazy heart flow together.
js_124: A drinking party opens at the home of Cho, a classmate turned tax-office chief.
js_125: The music teacher Ha In-suk sings the popular song Tears of Mokpo.
js_126: Mr. Park, a guileless junior who loves Ha In-suk, is there too.
js_127: The protagonist feels pity for Ha In-suk, who longs to go to Seoul.
js_129: The long seawall reaching to the sea
js_130: The protagonist and Ha In-suk walk together along the seawall to the sea.
js_131: On the seawall they come upon the corpse of a bar woman who took her own life.
js_132: The two spend a night together and grow closer.
js_133: A promise to take her to Seoul passes between them.
js_136: A telegram from his wife arrives, urging him to hurry back to Seoul.
js_137: He tears up the letter he wrote to Ha In-suk, never sending it.
js_138: The protagonist leaves Mujin and returns to the reality of Seoul.
js_139: Looking at the signpost, he feels a deep shame.
js_141: A Journey to Mujin, and after
js_142: It becomes the peak of the sensory style called a revolution of sensibility.
js_143: In 1967 it was also made into the film Mist, with a screenplay by Kim Seung-ok.
js_144: It’s loved enough that the Kim Seung-ok Literary Award was established in his honor.
js_145: The question of shame between reality and the ideal still holds today.
js_146: The night she sang Tears of Mokpo
js_147: Ha In-suk, who majored in vocal music at a Seoul conservatory, can’t refuse the tax chief’s request and sings the popular song Tears of Mokpo at the party. It’s a night when talent loses its place.
js_148: It makes you think about what a person gives up when the talent they hold loses its place to stand.
js_150: On the seawall to the sea, the protagonist passes indifferently by the corpse of a bar woman who killed herself. It’s a scene where the death and emptiness hanging over the foggy city surface, chill and clear.
js_151: It asks again what a heart, gone numb before another’s death, has actually lost.
js_153: The protagonist tears up, himself, the letter he wrote to Ha In-suk promising to take her to Seoul — never sending it. It’s a moment of self-deception, folding away sincerity before reality.
js_154: It makes you think about the promises we fail to keep and the shame they leave behind.
js_155: Why might we need a hazy, fog-like time of escape?
js_156: Think about both what the days in Mujin gave the protagonist and what they took away.
js_157: Like Ha In-suk, whose talent lost its place, what do we give up as we live?
js_158: Weigh the sorrow in the scene where she, a vocal-music major, sings a popular tune.
js_159: When he makes a promise he can’t keep and tears up the letter, where does Yun Hui-jung’s shame come from?
js_160: Read it as shame that comes from honestly facing the self that chose reality.
js_161: 🌫️ One who saw through the fog
js_162: You read A Journey to Mujin perfectly
js_163: 🚌 One who traveled all of Mujin
js_164: You’ve got almost all of the core
js_165: 🎶 One who heard the Tears of Mokpo
js_166: You’ve got the framework — one more time!
js_167: 🌁 A traveler lost in the fog
js_168: The more you repeat, the higher your score
